perturbation.original_text: Each Party, for itself and its associates as defined below, represents and warrants that it shall not conduct business with any sources or contacts, or said source's or contact's associates as defined below, that are originally made known and/or available by another Party hereto, at any time or in any manner, without the express written permission (not to be unreasonably withheld) of the Party who made the source(s) known and/or available.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party, for itself and its associates as defined below, will endeavor to avoid conducting business with any sources or contacts, or said source's or contact's associates as defined below, that are originally made known and/or available by another Party hereto, at any time or in any manner, without seeking permission from the Party who made the source(s) known and/or available.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not conduct business... without express written permission' to 'will endeavor to avoid conducting business... without seeking permission' weakens the obligation. 'Endeavor to avoid' is vague and subjective, and 'seeking permission' does not require obtaining it, making circumvention easier and the original intent less enforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law regarding breach of contract and good faith
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in contracts. The modified language introduces ambiguity that could allow a party to claim they 'endeavored to avoid' a conflict while still indirectly benefiting from a contact introduced by the other party, arguably breaching the implied duty of good faith if their actions undermine the contract's original purpose.

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original obligation, creating ambiguity regarding a party's responsibility. The original text states that the parties 'shall not conduct business... without express written permission', while the modified text states that the parties 'will endeavor to avoid conducting business... without seeking permission'. The law explanation suggests that this could violate the implied duty of good faith. The Justia snippet (13 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 1304 (2022)) states that 'Every contract or duty within this title imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By changing the language to 'endeavor to avoid' and 'seeking permission', the modified text allows a party to potentially circumvent the original intent of the contract. This introduces an ambiguous legal obligation, which is the definition of the specified Perturbation Type.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties will maintain complete confidentiality regarding this Agreement and all transactions occurring thereunder, each other's business, business sources and affiliates and each other's propriety knowledge and know-how, and will disclose such information only pursuant to the express written permission of the party who made such information available save where such information deemed to be in the public domain or under the order of a competent Court or Government Agency.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties will generally maintain confidentiality regarding this Agreement and transactions, and may disclose information if they believe it is necessary, except for information demonstrably in the public domain or required by legal authority.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will maintain complete confidentiality' to 'will generally maintain confidentiality' and replacing 'only pursuant to the express written permission' with 'if they believe it is necessary' introduces subjectivity. The addition of the word 'demonstrably' weakens the caveat for public information. This makes the confidentiality obligation unclear and open to interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: PUTSA protects trade secrets. The modified clause weakens the protection of proprietary information. If a party discloses a trade secret believing it 'necessary' without express permission, it may be more difficult to prove a misappropriation claim under PUTSA because the agreement no longer provides a clear definition of how confidential information should be handled.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5302 (2022) - Definitions :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5302 - Definitions Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5302 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5302. Definitions. The following words and phrases when used in this chapter shall have the meanings given to them in this section unless the context clearly indicates otherwise: "Improper means." Includes, but is not limited to, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation." Includes: (1) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. "Person." A natural person, corporation,
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens confidentiality obligations. The original text mandates 'complete confidentiality,' which is a strong obligation. The changed text uses 'generally maintain confidentiality,' which weakens this obligation. The original text requires 'express written permission' for disclosure, creating a clear standard. The changed text allows disclosure if the parties 'believe it is necessary,' creating a subjective standard. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'misappropriation' as including 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' The original contract aligns with this by requiring express written consent. The changed contract, by allowing disclosure based on a party's belief of necessity, potentially contradicts this, as it opens the door to disclosure without express consent, increasing the risk of misappropriation as defined in the statute. The use of 'demonstrably' also adds a burden of proof that wasn't present in the original, making the exception for public information broader and less precise.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party represents, warrants and covenants that all information furnished by said party, or to be furnished by said Party, or to any other Party or Parties hereto is, or will be, true, complete, correct and accurate to best of said Party's knowledge, ability and belief.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party represents that information furnished by said party is believed to be generally accurate.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'warrants and covenants', 'complete', 'correct', and 'accurate' significantly weakens the obligation for truthfulness. Replacing 'to the best of said Party's knowledge, ability and belief' with 'believed to be generally accurate' makes the standard subjective and difficult to prove a breach of. This reduces the legal protection against misrepresentation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law regarding fraud and misrepresentation
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law, following the Restatement (Second) of Torts § 525, holds parties liable for fraudulent misrepresentation. The original clause ensured the party was accountable for the accuracy of the information shared during the contract period. The modified text allows a party to avoid liability by claiming they 'believed' the information was 'generally accurate', even if it was false, incomplete, or misleading, making it harder to prove fraudulent misrepresentation because the standard of 'belief' is very subjective.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: negligence | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you negligence Negligence is the failure to behave with the level of care that a reasonable person would have exercised under the same circumstances. Either a person’s actions or omissions of actions can be found negligent. The omission of actions is considered negligent only when the person had a duty to act (e.g., a duty to help someone because of one’s own previous conduct). Negligence is a foundational concept of tort law. Some primary factors to consider in ascertaining whether a person’s conduct lacks reasonable care are the foreseeable likelihood that the conduct would result in harm, the foreseeable severity of the harm, and the burden of precautions necessary to eliminate or reduce the risk of harm. The following five elements may typically be required to prove negligence: The existence of a legal duty that the defendant owed the plaintiff Defendant’s breach of that duty Harm to the plaintiff Defendant’s actions are the proximate cause of harm to the plaintiff Defendant’s actions are the cause-in-fact of harm to the plaintiff Legal Duty of Care Generally, members of society have entered a social contract that includes a duty to not cause harm to others. When considering legal duty as an element of negligence, there is a duty to act reasonably. When a jury is asked to determine if a defendant’s actions were reasonable, the standard is what a reasonable person would have done in the defendant’s situation. The following is a non-exhaustive list of ways to determine if the defendant owed a duty of care to the plaintiff: The defendant engaged in the creation of risk which resulted in the plaintiff’s harm Voluntary undertaking: the defendant volunteered to protect the plaintiff from harm, possibly preventing others from protecting the plaintiff Knowledge: the defendant knows or should know that their conduct will harm the plaintiff Business/voluntary relationships: certain relationships—such as business owner and customer, innkeeper and guest, landowner and lessee, doctor and patient—create duties of care Breach of Duty of Care Some courts in the United States use the Hand Formula (created by Judge Learned Hand in United States v. Carroll Towing ) to determine if there was a breach of the duty of care to the plaintiff. If B < PL, then the defendant breached the duty of care. B=burden of taking precautions P=probability of loss L=severity of loss (personal loss, not societal loss) If the burden of taking such precautions is less than the probability of injury multiplied by the severity of the resulting injury, then the defending party breached their duty of care to the plaintiff and may be liable for the plaintiff’s injuries, if the remaining elements of negligence can be proven. This formula considers economic costs to the defendant in its approach to assessing liability. The idea is not to eliminate the risk of harm entirely, for complete elimination of risk would result in a high economical burden for the defendant that would then trickle down to costs for the consumer. Instead, the defendant is encouraged to take precautions that reduce either the probability of harm or severity of possible harm, such that their burden is less than the probability of harm multiplied by the severity of potential harm. Harm to the Plaintiff Typically, harm to the plaintiff must be either bodily harm or harm to property ( personal property or real property ). Harm that is solely economic usually will not satisfy this element in proving negligence. Some states recognize emotional distress as harm to the plaintiff, even if the harm is purely mental and not physical. Proximate Cause To satisfy proximate cause, the defendant’s breach of the duty of care must be the actual cause of the harm to the plaintiff. One way to assess proximate cause is to evaluate the foreseeability of the defendant’s actions resulting in harm. For example, if the d
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original obligation for truthfulness, creating a legal contradiction. The original text ensured accuracy to the best of the party's knowledge, ability, and belief, while the modified version only requires a belief that the information is 'generally accurate.' This shift introduces ambiguity and subjectivity, making it difficult to prove misrepresentation. The provided law snippet discusses negligence and the duty of care. While it does not directly address misrepresentation, the principle of acting reasonably is outlined. By weakening the requirement for accurate information and making it easier to avoid liability through a subjective belief standard, the changed text undermines the expected duty of care by reducing the burden on the party to ensure information accuracy. This alteration could allow a party to provide misleading information without facing legal repercussions, thus contradicting the spirit of reasonable care.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of circumvention by the "Parties" involved in this transaction, either directly or indirectly, it is agreed and guaranteed that a monetary penalty will be paid by the person or persons engaged in or circumvention. This payment will additionally include all reasonable legal expenses incurred by the aggrieved party.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of potential circumvention by the Parties involved in this transaction, the parties may discuss a possible monetary consideration.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'it is agreed and guaranteed that a monetary penalty will be paid' with 'the parties may discuss a possible monetary consideration' makes the penalty discretionary and uncertain. Removing the guarantee and turning it into a discussion weakens the enforceability of the anti-circumvention clause. This turns a clear obligation into a suggestion.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law regarding contract damages for breach of contract
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law allows for damages to be awarded in the case of a breach of contract. The original clause ensured the party was accountable for the actions that resulted in a circumvention. The modified text turns the obligation into a suggestion of a discussion, and nothing more. There is no longer a clear directive that allows for damages to be pursued should a contract breach occur. The absence of clear financial penalties discourages adherence to the contract's intent, undermining the principle of providing remedies for breach of contract, according to Pennsylvania law.
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the original contractual obligation regarding circumvention penalties, transforming a guaranteed payment into a potential discussion. This directly undermines the principle that a breach of contract should result in a remedy, as described in the Law Explanation. The original text created a clear obligation for payment in the event of circumvention. By changing this to a mere discussion, the modified text removes the mandatory nature of the penalty, making it less enforceable and therefore contradicting the need for damages in the case of a breach of contract. The absence of scraped snippets doesn't negate this as the Law Explanation specifies the undermining of remedies for breach of contract.
